description_long: This object consists of three large, interlocking concrete structures known as tetrapods, commonly used as barriers in urban and coastal environments. Each tetrapod has a robust, geometric form with three thick, angular arms radiating from a central column, providing stability and resistance to movement. The surfaces are rough and matte, with visible textural variations and slight color differences among the three units, ranging from light gray to dark gray and brownish-gray. The tops of the tetrapods are flat and slightly beveled, while the bases are wide and stable, designed to rest securely on the ground. There are no visible reflective markings, chains, or connectors, and the overall construction appears solid and heavy, likely made from reinforced concrete. These objects are typically used to control vehicle access, prevent unauthorized entry, or serve as anti-ram barriers in urban settings.
description: A group of three large, interlocking concrete tetrapod barriers with rough, matte surfaces in varying shades of gray and brown. Each has three thick arms and a flat, beveled top, designed for stability and access control.
description_view_0: Front view showing the aligned tops of the three tetrapods, highlighting their flat, beveled caps and the symmetry of their arms.
description_view_1: Angled side view displaying the three tetrapods' arms radiating outward, with clear visibility of their geometric structure and surface texture.
description_view_2: Rear view emphasizing the bases and the arrangement of the tetrapods, with the brownish-gray unit most prominent in the foreground.
description_view_3: Oblique view showing the side and top surfaces of all three tetrapods, revealing their color differences and robust construction.
